original_abstract: While Ecuador has formally recognized the necessity of upgrading infrastructure and resources to foster educational inclusion, the country continues to struggle with practical implementation when compared to the advancements made by developed nations. This study sought to map international regulations and best practices concerning inclusive educational environments to identify critical knowledge gaps and potential adaptation strategies for the Ecuadorian context. Guided by PRISMA-ScR protocols, we conducted an exploratory theoretical review of academic databases and official policy documents. The analysis focused on 16 scientific articles and 11 regulatory documents from the United States, Finland, Canada, and Japan. Our findings indicate that Ecuador’s efforts are hindered by a significant gap between policy and practice, particularly regarding physical accessibility and specialized resources. The results underscore an urgent need to establish rigorous national accessibility standards, increase investment in assistive technologies, and provide sustained teacher training. Furthermore, the study identifies a self-perpetuating cycle where the failure to enforce inclusive regulations reinforces institutional inertia and deepens educational inequality. We conclude that meaningful reform in Ecuador requires a holistic approach that simultaneously addresses regulatory frameworks, resource management, and the underlying educational culture.
humanized_abstract: Ecuador has recognized the need to improve the infrastructure and resources that will enable educational inclusion, a goal that is much more difficult to achieve in this country than in the developed countries. Therefore, the purpose of this study was to map the international regulations and good practices of inclusive educational spaces that allow us to identify the knowledge gaps that must be addressed in Ecuador and the possible strategies to replicate these practices. Following the PRISMA-ScR guidelines, an exploratory theoretical review was carried out in the scientific literature of the most important academic databases and official policy documents on this topic. As a result, 16 scientific articles and 11 policy documents from the United States of America, Finland, Canada and Japan were analyzed. The results showed that, in Ecuador, there is a policy implementation gap, as well as a lack of physical accessibility and adequate resources. Therefore, the main contributions of this study are: - To propose that the National Organism that regulates accessibility in educational institutions be more precise and strict with regard to accessibility criteria - To increase investment in assistive technologies in educational institutions, as well as to promote their use in a more effective way - To provide teachers with ongoing training and that it be on the use of technology in teaching, so that it is more inclusive and thus has a positive impact on the educational process It was also found that there is a vicious cycle that must be broken, which consists of the lack of enforcement of the regulations that perpetuate the bureaucratic resistance of educational institutions and, therefore, the educational exclusivity that affects the students with disabilities. The achievement of effective educational reform in Ecuador will depend on how the new regulations are designed, how the management of the infrastructure and resources is carried out, and on the prevailing educational culture.
